article_title: I ditched Cloudflare DNS—its replacement exposed what my devices were secretly doing in the background
core_thesis: While Cloudflare DNS is fast, simple, and reliable, its lack of granular control and visibility into DNS requests can leave users unaware of hidden device telemetry and security threats. The article argues that NextDNS offers a superior alternative by providing detailed DNS logs, customizable security filters, and privacy controls, all through an easy-to-use interface, making it ideal for users who want more oversight and proactive protection without the complexity of self-hosted solutions like Pi-hole.
detailed_summary: The article begins by acknowledging Cloudflare DNS's strengths: speed, simplicity, reliability, and support for DNS over HTTPS (DoH). It notes that Cloudflare offers basic filtering via 1.1.1.2 (malware blocking) and 1.1.1.3 (adult content blocking), but these are limited and lack granularity. The author then introduces NextDNS as a more powerful alternative that provides a detailed dashboard showing every DNS request from every device on the network, including the domain, frequency, server location, and originating device. This visibility can reveal hidden telemetry or data collection by apps. NextDNS also offers 13 security filters (e.g., typosquatting, cryptojacking, malicious domains) and extensive privacy controls (ad/tracker blocking, custom blocklists, parental controls with time limits and VPN blocking). The service has a free tier (300,000 queries/month) and an unlimited plan for $2/month or $20/year, which the author considers reasonable compared to the cost and effort of setting up a Pi-hole. The article concludes that NextDNS is a comprehensive security and privacy package that is easy to use and highly customizable, making it a strong choice for users who want more control than Cloudflare provides.

entities.name: Cloudflare
entities.role: DNS service provider; the article compares its basic filtering to NextDNS's advanced features.

entities.name: NextDNS
entities.role: Alternative DNS service offering granular control, detailed logs, and customizable security/privacy filters.

entities.name: Pi-hole
entities.role: Self-hosted DNS filtering tool; mentioned as a DIY alternative that requires more effort and cost.

entities.name: Nick Lewis
entities.role: Author of the article; editor at How-To Geek with a background in programming and homelabbing.

key_data_points: Cloudflare DNS (1.1.1.1) is fast, reliable, and supports DoH, but offers only basic filtering via 1.1.1.2 (malware) and 1.1.1.3 (adult content), with no per-device visibility or custom blocklists. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: limited visibility means you might miss C2 traffic or data exfiltration; switching to NextDNS can help detect such activity.)

NextDNS provides a dashboard showing all DNS requests, including domain, frequency, server location, and device, which can reveal hidden telemetry or unauthorized data collection. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this visibility can be used to identify beaconing or suspicious outbound connections during reconnaissance.)

NextDNS offers 13 security filters, including protection against typosquatting, homograph attacks, cryptojacking, and malicious domains. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: these filters can be bypassed or tested; understanding them helps in evading detection.)

NextDNS allows custom blocklists, ad/tracker blocking, parental controls (time limits, category blocking, VPN/TOR blocking), and global safe search. (🟢 Relevant to Homelab/DevOps: can be integrated into network-wide filtering for lab environments.)

NextDNS free tier includes 300,000 queries/month; unlimited plan costs $2/month or $20/year. (🟢 Relevant to Student: affordable option for personal use; can be used to secure home network on a budget.)

Setting up a Pi-hole costs at least $20 in components and requires more hands-on management than NextDNS. (🟢 Relevant to Homelab: Pi-hole is still a valid DIY option for those who prefer self-hosting and full control.)
methodology_steps: 1. Identify your need for DNS control: If you want basic speed and simplicity, Cloudflare is sufficient. If you need granular visibility and filtering, consider NextDNS.
2. Sign up for NextDNS: Visit nextdns.io and create an account. The free tier is enough to test the service.
3. Configure your router or device: Set the DNS server to the NextDNS provided IP addresses (or use the app). For routers, change the WAN DNS settings to point to NextDNS.
4. Explore the dashboard: After setup, log into the NextDNS console to view live DNS logs, see which devices are making requests, and identify any suspicious or unwanted traffic.
5. Apply security filters: In the console, enable the 13 security filters (e.g., malicious domains, typosquatting, cryptojacking) to block threats proactively.
6. Customize privacy controls: Add blocklists for ads, trackers, or specific categories. Create custom blocklists for particular domains or services.
7. Set up parental controls (optional): Use time limits, category blocking, and VPN/TOR blocking to restrict device usage.
8. Monitor and adjust: Regularly review logs and adjust filters as needed. If you exceed the free tier's 300,000 queries, consider upgrading to the unlimited plan.
conclusions: Cloudflare DNS is excellent for users who prioritize speed and simplicity, but its limited filtering and lack of per-device visibility make it unsuitable for those who want to monitor and control network traffic in detail.

NextDNS provides a comprehensive, user-friendly alternative that combines DNS resolution with advanced security and privacy features, making it a strong choice for users who want more control without the complexity of self-hosted solutions like Pi-hole.

The article's recommendation is supported by the detailed feature comparison and the author's personal experience; however, some commenters note that Cloudflare's Gateway service (part of Zero Trust) can offer similar functionality, and that NextDNS may be slower in some regions.

For Red Teaming, the visibility provided by NextDNS can be a double-edged sword: it helps defenders detect malicious activity, but understanding its filtering logic can help attackers evade detection. For homelab enthusiasts, NextDNS offers a low-maintenance alternative to Pi-hole.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity, homelabbing, and programming. As a Red Teamer, you can leverage NextDNS's detailed logs to simulate detection and evasion scenarios, or use it to monitor your own lab for unexpected outbound connections. For your homelab, NextDNS provides a simple, cost-effective way to add network-wide filtering without dedicating a Raspberry Pi or VM. The article also touches on the trade-offs between managed services and self-hosted solutions, which is valuable for your DevOps mindset. As a student, the affordable pricing and easy setup make it a practical tool for securing your personal devices and learning about DNS-level security controls.
extra_advice: Consider setting up NextDNS in a test environment and intentionally triggering its security filters (e.g., by visiting known malicious domains or using a VPN) to understand its detection capabilities. This hands-on experience will help you both as a defender and as an attacker looking to bypass such controls. Additionally, you could write a script to parse NextDNS logs and automatically flag suspicious patterns, combining your programming skills with network security.
